behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: Out-of-stock filtering requires reading the actual availability status from a persistent data source. A unit test with mocked products could pass even if the code queries the wrong field, reads availability from the wrong source, or doesn't consult the availability check at all. Integration testing against a real database ensures the filtering logic is correctly wired to the actual availability data.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test database with products in different availability states (in-stock and out-of-stock). Generate recommendations and verify that only in-stock products appear, including products marked out-of-stock that have high relevance scores. Run multiple times to ensure consistent filtering.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: User preferences are stored in a persistent system. Mocking both the preference service and product catalog masks real failure modes: wrong field names in preference objects, preference queries not being executed, or stale preference caches. Integration testing with real preference records in a test database ensures preferences are correctly loaded and applied to filter results.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test user with blocked categories stored in a test database. Populate the catalog with products across various categories including the blocked ones. Request recommendations and verify no products from blocked categories appear. Test with multiple blocked categories and products with high relevance scores in those categories.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: The ranking algorithm consumes real browsing history from a data source, not just a ranking formula. Unit testing with hardcoded browsing patterns could pass even if the algorithm uses the wrong time window for 'recent', applies incorrect weighting, or queries browsing history from the wrong table. Integration testing with realistic browsing history in a test database verifies the algorithm correctly weights temporal browsing signals.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test user with documented browsing history (specific categories, timestamps) stored in a test database. Populate products across multiple categories. Generate recommendations and verify products matching recent browsing appear higher in the ranked list than products with no browsing signal. Include products with tied ranking scores to test tie-breaking.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: Stable ordering requires real database query semantics. A unit test with mocked data would always return results in the same order, masking real non-determinism from missing ORDER BY clauses, non-deterministic ranking tie-breaking in the database, or other persistence-layer issues. Only Integration testing with a real database reveals whether ordering is actually stable across multiple requests.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test database with many products having varying relevance scores, including ties. Request page 2 of recommendations twice without modifying data and verify identical product IDs in identical order both times. Test different page numbers and edge cases (boundary products, last page). Repeat across multiple recommendation requests.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before, avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: The serendipity logic must be wired into the ranking algorithm and must actually surface novel categories in returned results. Mocking user browsing history and categories could pass even if the serendipity logic is missing entirely or positioned incorrectly (e.g., serendipity item only appears on page 3). Integration testing with real category data and documented browsing history in a database ensures the serendipity mechanism functions.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test user with browsing history limited to specific categories stored in a test database. Populate the catalog with products from both familiar and unfamiliar categories (unfamiliar products can have lower relevance scores). Request recommendations and verify at least one product from an unvisited category appears in the results. Test with multiple browsing patterns.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: The fallback logic must correctly detect missing user history and substitute popularity metrics from a data source. A unit test with mocked data could pass even if popularity metrics aren't being fetched, if the fallback logic is missing, or if the query returns empty. Integration testing with a real database ensures the fallback mechanism is wired correctly and that recommendations actually return populated, popularity-ranked results.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test user with no purchase or browsing history. Populate the catalog with products assigned popularity scores stored in a test database. Request recommendations and verify that results are returned (not empty) and ranked by popularity. Verify that the fallback path is actually being executed by comparing results to a user with history.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: Click tracking must persist data to a permanent store. Mocking the tracking service could pass even if database writes fail, data is corrupted, or position calculations have off-by-one errors. Integration testing with a real database is necessary to verify that clicks are actually persisted with correct field values (product ID matches recommendations, position matches list order, timestamp is valid).
behaviors.test_description: Generate recommendations for a test user. Simulate clicks on products at various positions in the results. Query the test database to verify each click is persisted with: correct product ID, accurate position matching the original list order, and realistic timestamp. Test clicks on products at different page positions and multiple clicks.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: Performance regressions like O(n²) algorithms, missing database indexes, or inefficient query patterns can be caught with Integration testing using a real test database at scale (100k products). This infrastructure reveals realistic database I/O and algorithmic complexity without requiring production deployment. However, absolute SLA validation (200ms on production hardware) requires System testing; Integration provides the minimum to catch algorithmic efficiency bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test database with 100,000 realistic products and user data. Trigger recommendation requests and measure end-to-end response time including database queries and ranking algorithm. Verify response time stays under 200ms (or benchmark against test hardware baseline). Profile execution to identify O(n²) algorithms, missing indexes, or inefficient queries. Test with multiple concurrent requests.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: This requires multi-step state changes across a persistent system: generate recommendations, record a purchase, regenerate recommendations, verify ranking changed. Unit testing with mocked state always passes, but masks real failures: purchase events not being recorded, deprioritization logic missing entirely, or purchase data not being visible to the ranking algorithm due to caching or transaction issues. Integration testing with a real database ensures the full flow works correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test database with user preferences and products. Generate recommendations and record the position/rank of a specific product. Simulate a purchase of that product in the database. Generate recommendations again and verify the purchased product has a lower rank or does not appear. Test with multiple purchases and verify consistent deprioritization behavior across multiple recommendation cycles.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: Determinism requires that database queries return consistent results, the ranking algorithm contains no non-deterministic elements (unsorted sets, floating-point rounding variation, random number generators without seeds, timestamps), and system time doesn't leak into results. Unit testing with hardcoded mock data always appears deterministic, masking real non-determinism from database cursors, the ranking algorithm, or result construction. Integration testing with real database queries and algorithm execution reveals true determinism behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test database with user data, browsing history, and products. Request recommendations twice with no intervening user activity and verify both requests return identical products in identical order. Repeat multiple times and test edge cases like products with tied ranking scores. Include performance profiling to ensure results aren't deterministic only by accident (e.g., because algorithm is too slow to vary).
